input.background: Thirteen patients with chronic skin disorders were studied in an open trial to assess the effect of aspirin on pruritis. No treatment was given on nights one or two, aspirin (900 mg) was given on the third and fourth nights, and no treatment was given on the fifth and sixth nights. On each night scratching was measured by a limb movement meter, and an assessment of itching was made by the patient the following morning using a 10 cm linear analogue scale. For each patient the data were averaged over each two day period. The results were presented in the paper (Daly and Shuster, 1986) as means (SE): The complete comment on this table was as follows: There was interindividual and intraindividual variation in itch and scratch throughout the study, but no consistent change in either measurement was recorded during aspirin treatment.
input.question: What can you say about the distribution across patients of the nocturnal limb movements score?

output.answer: The standard deviations are obtained as SE × √13, or 36.1, 26.7 and 19.8 for the three sets of observations. The data are thus skewed, especially before treatment, because the SD is more than half the mean.
